title: Parameters and Arguments
description: The precise difference between a parameter and an argument, default parameter values, and matching a function call correctly.
objectives: Distinguish parameters (in the definition) from arguments (at the call site)
Match a function call's arguments to its parameters correctly
Understand why argument order and count must match the function's signature

rows: A placeholder name for an expected input
The actual value handed to the function for that call

sections.text: These two words are often used loosely as if interchangeable, but the distinction is a common interview question: parameters are the placeholders a function declares; arguments are the real values supplied when it's called.
multiply(4, 5) — the first argument, 4, fills a; the second, 5, fills b. Arguments are matched to parameters by position, in order.

sections.code:
multiply(4);       // ERROR — missing an argument for b
multiply(4, 5, 6); // ERROR — too many arguments

sections.text: A function expecting two parameters must be called with exactly two arguments (unless default values are involved — see below). Too few or too many is a compilation error, not something silently ignored.

Arguments fill parameters strictly by position — the first argument always goes to the first parameter, regardless of what the values conceptually "mean." Swapping the order of arguments swaps which value lands in which parameter.
A default parameter value is used automatically when the caller doesn't supply an argument for that parameter.
power(5) is valid even though power technically has two parameters — exponent falls back to its default, 2, when no second argument is given. power(5, 3) overrides that default explicitly.

sections.code: int power(int exponent = 2, int base); // ERROR — default before a non-default parameter

sections.text: Once a parameter has a default value, every parameter after it must also have one — a non-default parameter can't follow a default one. This makes sense given how calls work: the caller supplies arguments left to right, so a gap in the middle would be ambiguous.

sections.title: A form with blank fields and pre-filled ones
sections.text: A parameter is like a labeled blank on a form — "Name: ____". An argument is what you actually write in that blank when filling it out. Some fields might come pre-filled with a common default (like a pre-checked checkbox), which you can leave as-is or override — exactly like a default parameter value.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Using the terms parameter and argument interchangeably in an interview — they're related but distinct: parameter = the placeholder, argument = the real value.
Passing arguments in the wrong order — position matters, the first argument always fills the first parameter.
Calling a function with too few or too many arguments — a compilation error, unless the missing ones have default values.
Giving a non-default parameter after a default one — the language requires all defaulted parameters to be trailing.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is the difference between a parameter and an argument?
questions.answer: A parameter is the placeholder variable declared in a function's own definition or declaration; an argument is the actual value supplied at the call site for that parameter.

questions.question: How are arguments matched to parameters?
questions.answer: By position — the first argument fills the first parameter, the second argument fills the second parameter, and so on, in order.

questions.question: What is a default parameter value?
questions.answer: A value a parameter uses automatically when the caller doesn't provide an argument for it.

questions.question: Can a non-default parameter follow a default parameter?
questions.answer: No — once a parameter has a default value, every parameter after it must also have a default value.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: A parameter is the placeholder name a function declares for an expected input, while an argument is the actual value supplied when the function is called — arguments are matched to parameters by position, in order, and the count must match unless default values are involved. A default parameter value is used automatically when the caller omits that argument, letting a function be called with fewer arguments than it technically declares. Once a parameter has a default value, every parameter after it must have one too, since arguments are always supplied left to right.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Parameters and Arguments
sections.text: Parameter = the blank on the form. Argument = what you write in it. Default value = a pre-filled blank you can leave or override.
